coredata.dc:title: Switchable two-color graphene quantum dot as a promising fluorescence probe to highly sensitive pH detection and bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiyi, Li
Huahua, Zhang
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots have been widely applied in biosensing, fluorescence imaging, biomedicine, energy storage and conversion and catalysis, but design and synthesis of polychromatic graphene quantum dot with high luminous efficiency still faces great challenges. The study reports synthesis of histidine, serine and pentaethylenehexamine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot (HSPB-GQD) via one-step pyrolysis. The resulting HSPB-GQD consists of graphene sheets of 2–5 nm with carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino, imino and imidazole. Synergy of histidine, serine, pentaethylenehexamine and boron atoms improves the luminescence behavior. This realizes unique switchable two-color luminescence. UV excitation of 370 nm produces one strong blue fluorescence with the maximum emission wavelength of 455 nm and quantum yield of 72.34%. Vis. excitation of 480 nm produces one strong yellow fluorescence with the maximum emission wavelength of 560 nm and quantum yield of 72.59%. The multiple proton dissociation system constructed by nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing groups makes yellow fluorescence sensitive to environmental pH value. The intensity linearly increases with increasing pH in the range of 4.5–10.0. Organic molecules and inorganic ions do not interfere pH detection. HSPB-GQD as a promising fluorescence probe with negligible effect on cell viability was successfully applied to pH detection in biological and environmental water samples and cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Synthesis
Graphene quantum dot
Optical properties
pH probe
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), AFM image (B and C) and FTIR spectrum (D) of HSPB-GQD.
Total (A), C1s (B), N1s (C) and B1s XPS spectra of HSPB-GQD.

The fluorescence spectra (A) of HSPB-GQD at the excitation wavelength of 340 (a), 350 (b), 360 (c), 370 (d), 400 (e), 420 (f), 430 (g), 450 (h) and 480 nm (i) and the relationship curves (B) between peak fluorescence intensity and excitation wavelength (a) and between maximum emission wavelength and excitation wavelength (b).
Peak fluorescence intensities of GQD, H-GQD, HS-GQD, HSP-GQD and HSPB-GQD at 370 nm UV excitation and 480 nm Vis. excitation.
Fluorescence spectra (A) of HSPB-GQD in the BR buffer of pH 4.5 and 10 and the pH response reversibility (B) of HSPB-GQD between pH 4.5 and 10.

The fluorescence spectra (A) of HSPB-GQD under Vis excitation of 480 nm in a BR buffer of pH 4.5, 5, 5.5, 6, 6.5, 7, 7.5, 8, 8.5, 9, 9.5 and 10 (from top to bottom) and the relationship curve (B) of peak fluorescence intensity at 560 nm with pH value.
The change in peak fluorescence intensity (F/F0) after and before added interfere species.
Yellow CLSM images of the Hela cells incubated in HSPB-GQD for different times.
The results of detection pH values in biological and environmental water samples (n = 5).

row.entry: The pH value obtained by the proposed method
The pH value obtained by the pH meter

head.ce:title: Switchable two-color graphene quantum dot as a promising fluorescence probe to highly sensitive pH detection and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots have been widely applied in biosensing, fluorescence imaging, biomedicine, energy storage and conversion and catalysis, but design and synthesis of polychromatic graphene quantum dot with high luminous efficiency still faces great challenges. The study reports synthesis of histidine, serine and pentaethylenehexamine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot (HSPB-GQD) via one-step pyrolysis. The resulting HSPB-GQD consists of graphene sheets of 2–5 nm with carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino, imino and imidazole. Synergy of histidine, serine, pentaethylenehexamine and boron atoms improves the luminescence behavior. This realizes unique switchable two-color luminescence. UV excitation of 370 nm produces one strong blue fluorescence with the maximum emission wavelength of 455 nm and quantum yield of 72.34%. Vis. excitation of 480 nm produces one strong yellow fluorescence with the maximum emission wavelength of 560 nm and quantum yield of 72.59%. The multiple proton dissociation system constructed by nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing groups makes yellow fluorescence sensitive to environmental pH value. The intensity linearly increases with increasing pH in the range of 4.5–10.0. Organic molecules and inorganic ions do not interfere pH detection. HSPB-GQD as a promising fluorescence probe with negligible effect on cell viability was successfully applied to pH detection in biological and environmental water samples and cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dot (GQD) is one new member of graphene family and has cause broad scientific interest because of its special structure that endows GQD with special optical and electrical properties [1]. As a luminescent material with tunable photoluminescence, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and high photostability, GQD has been applied in many optical fields such as fluorescence detection [2–4], bioimaging [5], drug delivery [6], photovoltaic [7], light-emitting diode [8] and photocatalysis [9,10]. As a semiconductor with adjustable energy level, GQD has been applied in construction of electrode materials in lithium ion battery [11], supercapacitor [12], fuel cell [13] and catalysis in hydrogen reduction [14], oxygen reduction [15] and nitrogen reduction [16]. However, an ideal application strongly requires GQD can offer the matching structure and properties with their requirements. Design and synthesis of GQDs for meeting needs of further applications become an urgent problem to be solved in expanding its application fields.

Three solutions were developed for improving the optical and electrical properties of GQDs. The first solution is to adjust the size of graphene sheets. Due to the confinement effect, a smaller size of graphene sheets with a narrower size distribution often brings a higher luminous efficiency with a reduced electrical conductivity. Thus, changing the size of graphene sheets can effectively adjust the photoelectric properties of GQDs. Ha et al. reported synthesis of GQD via ion-beam assisted chemical vapor deposition [17]. Firstly, the nano-sized Fe nanoparticle was prepared by diffusion and aggregation of Fe atoms. Then, it was employed as the catalyst for the reduction of CH4 gas in a high temperature and made GQDs. As Fe nanoparticles were completely removed under a high temperature, the resultant GQD product offers high purity and quality. In addition, the product has a narrow size distribution of graphene sheets and high crystallinity. In addition to the above bottom-up approach, many top-down approaches were also applied to adjust the size of graphene sheets such as the chemical cutting method [18] and aromatic polymerization growth method [19]. However, it is very difficult to accurately control the size of graphene sheets. The practical effect on regulating the properties of GQD is very limited, and it is not easy to realize a large-scale production. The second solution is to dope atoms into carbon framework of graphene sheets. The doping is to introduce special heteroatoms into carbon skeleton of graphene sheets such as nitrogen [20] and boron [21]. This leads to an obvious change in electronic structure and realizes the regulation of photoelectric properties. Minchul Ahn et al. reported synthesis of N-doped GQDs by microwave treatment of mixed citric acid with polyethyleneimine [22]. Based on the interaction with negatively charged gene, the positive N-doped GQD has been successfully applied to transfection of mRNA and p-DNA in COVID-19 diagnosis. The third solution is to functionalize GQDs. The solution received a more attention compared with controlling size of graphene sheets and doping atoms, because it can not only improve the photoelectric properties but also brings special functions to meet the needs of different applications. For example, the introduction of folic acid endows GQD with the ability to capture tumor cells [23]. The introduction of histidine makes GQD can form stable coordination bonds with transition metal ions. Thus, histidine-functionalized GQDs have been used as a linker to immobilize metal ions on graphene sheets [24]. Our group designed and synthesized a series of functionalized GQDs by introducing natural amino acids, including methionine [25], histidine [26], phenylalanine [27], valine [28], glutamic acid [29], serine [30], serine and histidine [31], glutamic acid and histidine [32], serine and histidine [33]. These investigations demonstrate that the properties of GQDs depend on the structure and properties of the amino acid used in the synthesis. The resultant functionalized GQDs were successfully applied in fluorescence detection [34], electrochemical sensor [35], energy storage device [36], catalysis and emulsion polymerization [37]. In addition, the previous work also found that synergistic effect of multiple functional groups can further improve the properties of GQDs [38]. In spite of these advances, design and synthesis of polychromatic graphene quantum dot with high luminous efficiency still faces great challenges up to now.

The study reports synthesis of histidine, serine and pentaethylenehexamine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot (HSPB-GQD) via one-step pyrolysis. HSPB-GQD shows an unique fluorescence behavior because of synergy of histidine, serine, pentaethylenehexamine and boron. It has been successfully used as a promising optical probe for pH detection in biological and environmental water samples and cell imaging.

Citric acid (CA), serine (Ser), histidine (His), pentaethylenehexamine (PEHA) and boric acid (H3BO3) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution is prepared with 0.04 mol L-1 boric acid, phosphate and acetic acid and used with 0.2 mol L-1 NaOH solution on the acidity meter to the desired pH value. Other reagents are analytical grade and purchased from Sinopharm Shanghai reagent company.

The mixture, composing of citric acid (0.1 mol), histidine (0.1 mol), 0.2 g serine (0.02 mol), 0.1135 g (0.005 mol) pentaethylenehexamine and boric acid (0.02 mol), was transferred into the glass beaker of 200 mL. Added 5 mL of ultrapure water to dissolve the above compounds and then dried to remove free water in the reaction system. The beaker was placed in a high-temperature oven, heated at 180 °C for 3 h and cooled to ambient temperature in sequence. The resultant HSPB-GQD was dissolved in ultrapure water to form the HSPB-GQD aqueous solution of 100 mg·mL-1. To obtain solid HSPB-GQD, the solution was treated by dialysis with a dialysis bag (3500 Da) to remove unreacted raw materials and freeze-drying.

In 2 mL of centrifugal tube, added 100 μL of the HSPB-GQD aqueous solution (1.0 mg mL−1) and 500 μL of the BR buffer or sample solution and then stirred. The obtained solution was subject to fluorescence emission spectrum scanning and fluorescence intensity measuring on the fluorescence photometer. For the detection, the visible light of 480 nm was employed for the excitation, silt widths of excitation and emission were both 1.5 nm, and the voltage was 700 V.

Hela cells were seeded into 24-well plates with aseptic coverslips at concentration of 2 × 104 cells per well. After incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h, added the HSPB-GQD solution (20 μg mL−1 in the culture medium for blue fluorescence imaging or 20 μg mL−1 in the culture medium for yellow fluorescence imaging) to the Hela cells to replace the original medium. Followed by culturing for 24 h, washing with the PBS of pH 7.0 to remove free HSPB-GQD, adding 500 mL of 4% paraformaldehyde, incubation for 20 min, and washing with PBS to remove paraformaldehyde. The fixed cells were subject to fluorescence imaging on the confocal laser scanning microscopy under UV excitation of 390 nm and Vis. excitation of 480 nm.

HSPB-GQD was designed and synthesized by one-step pyrolysis of the mixed citric acid with histidine, serine, pentaethylenehexamine and boric acid at 180 °C for 3 h (shown in Fig. 1 ). The molar ratios of these reagents in synthesis of HSPB-GQDs were optimized and the results were shown in Fig. s1. The optimized molar ratios are 1.0 for histidine/citric acid (His/CA), 0.2 for serine/citric acid (Ser/CA), 0.05 for pentaethylenehexamine/citric acid (PEHA/CA) and 0.2 for boric acid/citric acid (H3BO3/CA). In the design, citric acid acts as one carbon source to make small graphene sheets. Due to unique structure and chemical properties, citric acid can be transformed into two-dimensional graphene sheet composing of sp2-carbon atoms by intermolecular condensation and subsequent carbonization in high temperature [39]. Serine, histidine and pentaethylenehexamine act as functional reagents to introduce special functional groups at the edge of graphene sheets. As one serine molecule contains one aliphatic hydroxyl group, and one pentaethylenehexamine molecule includes two amino groups and four imino groups, their introduction will increase the charge density of graphene sheets due to a strong electron-donating effect of the above groups. This brings an obviously improved luminous efficiency [40]. Different from serine and pentaethylenehexamine, one histidine molecule contains one imidazolyl group with a large electron conjugation system, its introduction expands electron delocalization range of GQDs. This will cause a red shift in fluorescence emission [41]. In addition, histidine, serine and pentaethylenehexamine are rich in nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing groups. Their dissociation and protonation may produce a change in fluorescence behavior, indicating a sensitive fluorescence reponse to the environmental pH. Boric acid acts as one dopant to introduce boron atoms into graphene skeleton carbon structure. Different from nitrogen and oxygen, boron is an electron-deficient element. Therefore, doping boron atom can increase the electron mobility in graphene sheets and results in an increased luminous efficiency [42]. More importantly, synergy of histidine, serine, pentaethylenehexamine and borion achieves to a more specific improvement in the photoelectric properties to meet the needs of further applications in fluorescence detection and bioimaging.

The as-synthesized HSPB-GQD was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2 presents TEM image, AFM image and FTIR spectrum of HSPB-GQD. The HSPB-GQD is composed of one-dimensional carbon dots. The size of graphene sheets is in the range from 2 to 5 nm with an average size of 3.4 ± 0.12 nm. The result verifies the pyrolysis of mixed citric acid with histidine, serine, pentaethylenehexamine with boric acid at high temperature of 180 °C can form graphene sheets. The height of graphene sheets in HSPB-GQD is between 300 and 1100 pm, indicating that there are 1–4 layers of graphene oxide-like structure. There are several strong IR absorption peaks and bands on the FTIR spectrum. The IR absorption peak at 3435.3 cm−1 is attributed to symmetric stretching vibration of N-H and O-H bonds. Owing to forming hydrogen bonds between –NH and –OH groups, the IR absorption peak become wide and strong. The peak at 2911.6 cm−1 is attributed to IR absorption of saturated -C-H bonds. The peak at 1707.2 cm−1 is the IR absorption caused by stretching vibration of C = O bond in –COOH group. As HSPB-GQD contains the rich of –COOH groups, the peak is the strongest IR absorption peak on the FTIR spectrum. The absorption bands at 1399.3 cm−1 and 1227.9 cm−1 can be assigned to stretching vibration of the C-H and C-O bonds, verifying the presence of carbonyl and C-H groups. The FTIR spectrum also contains many weak absorption peaks between 1200 and 500 cm−1. Because these peaks are the characteristic IR absorption peaks of histidine, serine and pentaethylenehexamine. The fact demonstrates that many functional groups in histidine, serine and pentaethylenehexamine molecules were partly remained during the synthesis of HSPB-GQD.

Fig. 3 presents total, C1s, N1s and B1s XPS spectra of the as-synthesized HSPB-GQD. The total XPS spectrum includes four XPS peaks at 190, 280, 400 and 532 eV, which are corresponded to B1s, C1s, N1s and O1s. The result demonstrates that HSPB-GQD is composed of boron (B) carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O) elements. The C element comes from citric acid, histidine, serine and pentaethylenehexamine. The O element comes from citric acid, histidine, serine and boric acid. The N element come from citric acid, histidine, serine and pentaethylenehexamine. The B element only come from boric acid. The high resolution C1s XPS spectrum includes four XPS peaks at 281.9, 282.9, 284.3 and 286.1 eV, which are corresponded to C-B, C-C, C-O and C-N bonds. From Fig. 3B, we can see the C-C bond in HSPB-GQD gives a lower binding energy than that of the C-C bond in common graphene (284.6 eV). The reduce in binding energy is attributed to boron atom in carbon lattice. Boron is one kind of electron deficient elements, its strong electron-withdrawing effect will peel off the C-C bond in HSPB-GQD and leads to a decreased binding energy of C-C bond. The high resolution N1s XPS spectrum includes three XPS peaks at 397.24, 398.04 and 398.84 eV. The peak at 397.24 eV is assigned to the pyridinic N, the peak at 398.04 eV corresponds to amine N, and the peak at 398.84 eV corresponds to graphitic N. As the N element in HSPB-GQD mainly come from amino and imino groups in histidine, serine and pentaethylenehexamine, the peak intensity of amine N is obviously higher than that of pyridinic N and graphitic N. The high resolution B1s XPS spectrum contains three XPS peaks at 189.2, 189.6 and 190.1 eV. The peak at 189.2 eV corresponds to boron atoms bonding to nitrogen atom (B-N bond), which the B-N bonds were formed by the reaction of –NH- or –NH2 groups at the edge of graphene sheets with H3BO3. The peak at 189.6 eV corresponds to boron atoms binding to oxygen atoms (B-O bond). The formation of B-O bond is due to the condensation between –OH and –COOH groups at the edge of graphene sheets and H3BO3. The most intense peak at 190.1 eV corresponds to the structure of boron atoms bonding to carbon. This suggests a large fraction of boron atoms were bonded to the carbon atoms in the sp2-carbon network.

To study on the luminescence behavior, the fluorescence spectra of HSPB-GQD were measured at different excitation wavelengthes. Fig. 4 shows that the fluorescence emission strongly depends on the excitation wavelength. The relationship curve of peak fluorescence intensity with the excitation wavelength has two peaks at the excitations of 370 nm and 480 nm. At UV excitation of 370 nm, the HSPB-GQD produces the strongest blue fluorescence with the maximum emission wavelength of 455 nm. At Vis excitation of 480 nm, the HSPB-GQD produces the strongest yellow fluorescence with the maximum emission wavelength of 560 nm. Interestingly, the blue fluorescence has a similar intensity to the yellow fluorescence. The maximum quantum yield is 72.34% for blue fluorescence and 72.59% for yellow fluorescence. Thus, HSPB-GQD is one typical two-color luminescent material that can produce strong blue fluorescence and strong yellow fluorescence (shown in Fig. s2 ). The relationship cure between the maximum emission wavelength and the excitation wavelength can be devided into three sections, including two flat sections and one steep section. One flat section in the excitation wavelength range of 340–390 nm only causes a change of less than 10 nm in the maximum emission wavelength. In this range, all emissions are blue fluorescence. Another flat section in the excitation wavelength range of 400–520 nm also gives a small change in the maximum emission wavelength. In the range, all emissions are the yellow fluorescence. However, one steep section in the excitation wavelength range of 390–400 nm shows a significant change of more than 70 nm in the maximum emission wavelength. In the range, a small change in the excitation wavelength realizes the transformation of fluorescence emission from blue light to yellow light. Based on the characteristic, the HSPB-GQD can be designed as a switchable two-color luminescent material.

To understand the effect of GQD structure on the luminescence behavior, a series of graphene quantum dots were synthesized by the same procedure, including common GQD (GQD), histidine-functionalized GQD (H-GQD), histidine and serine-co-functionalized GQD (HS-GQD), histidine, serine and pentaethylenehexamine-co-functionalized GQD (HSP-GQD) and HSPB-GQD. Further, their luminescence behaviors were investigated by varying the excitation wavelength. The results were shown in Fig. s3 , Fig. s4
,
Fig. s5
,
Fig. s6 and Fig. 5 , respectively. The results demonstrate that the structure of GQD seriously influences on its luminescence behavior. Both the fluorescence intensity and the maximum emission wavelength depend on the excitation wavelength. For the GQD, the maximum emission wavelength linearly increases with increasing the excitation wavelength, but the fluorescence intensity reaches to the maximum value at the excitation of 370 nm and then rapidly decreases with the increase of excitation wavelength. As a result, the GQD gives one strong blue fluorescence emission at the excitation of 370 nm and a very weak yellow fluorescence emission at the excitation of 480 nm. H-GQD has a similar luminescence behavior with GQD, but its blue fluorescence at the excitation of 370 nm is much stronger than that of GQD. This demonstrates that the introduction of histidine can improve the blue fluorescence emission. However, HS-GQD shows a significant change in luminescence behavior. On the one hand, HS-GQD has an enhanced blue fluorescence emission and an enhanced yellow fluorescence emission. Especially yellow fluorescence emission, its intensity is much higher than blue fluorescence. On the other hand, HS-GQD shows a significat different relationship curve of the maximum emission wavelength with the excitation wavelength from GQD and H-GQD, but it is similar to HSP-GQD and HSPB-GQD. Compared with HS-GQD, HSP-GQD exhibits a significantly increased blue fluorescence emission and a slightly reduced yellow fluorescence emission. However, the yellow fluorescence was obviously increased in the HSPB-GQD. This demonstrates that the introduction of boron can improve blue fluorescence emission and yellow fluorescence emission. Among all GQDs studied, only HSPB-GQD can produce one strong blue fluorescence and one strong yellow fluorescence. Based on the above results, we suggest the unique luminescence behavior of HSPB-GQD is attributed to synergy of histidine, serine, pentaethylenehexamine and boron atom.

The fluorescence response of HSPB-GQD to pH value was investigated at Vis excitation of 480 nm. Fig. 6
A presents the fluorescence spectra of HSPB-GQD in the BR buffers in the range from pH 4.5 to 10. At an acidic condition of pH 4.5, HSPB-GQD shows a strong yellow fluorescence with the maximum emission peak at 560 nm. However, the fluorescence will be significantly reduced at an alkaline condition of pH 10. The change in fluorescence intensity is mainly attributed to the existence of nitrogen-containing groups, oxygen-containing groups and doped boron atom in HSPB-GQD. Because nitrogen atom in nitrogen-containing groups such as amino (–NH2) and imino (–NH-) contain the lone pair electron, the amino and imino groups are easy to be protonated to form –NH3 + and –NH+- groups in an acidic condition. The protonation weakens the transfer of lone pair electron to graphene sheet via the p-π conjugation [39]. This effectively hinders the energy transfer from nitrogen-containing groups to graphene sheet. As a result, graphene sheet and nitrogen-containing group at the edge of graphene sheets remain a relatively high electron density. This makes HSPB-GQD is easily excited by light and produce strong fluorescence emission. However, in a alkaline condition amino and imino in HSPB-GQD can be deprotonated to form free –NH2 and –NH- groups. Such a deprotonation enhances the transfer of lone pair electron to graphene sheet. Consequently, the graphene sheet shows an increased electron density and the nitrogen-containing groups gives a reduced electron density. In this case, HSPB-GQD is difficult to be excited by light and brings a fluorescence quenching. Similar to nitrogen-containing groups, the protonation of oxygen-containing groups such as –OH and –COOH in an acidic condition achieves to an increase in fluorescence intensity, and its deprotonation in a alkaline condition leads to a reduce in fluorescence intensity. Different from nitrogen and oxygen atoms, boron is a trivalent electron-deficient element. Thus, doping boron atoms into carbon lattice results in the producing high concentration of hole carriers. This enhances the electron-withdrawing ability of graphene sheet from peripheral functional groups. In general, the doped boron atoms will further amplify the protonation and deprotonation effects of HSPB-GQD in acidic condition and alkaline condition. This helps to improve the sensitivity of sensing pH value.

Good reversibility of pH probe is important to its practical applications. To test the reversibility , the acidity of HSPB-GQD solution in a BR buffer (1.0 mg mL−1) was adjusted to pH 4.5 by dropping 1.0 mol L-1 HCl solution under stirring and then subject to measure its peak fluorescence intensity at 560 nm. After that, the acidity was again adjusted to pH 10 by dropping 1.0 mol L-1 NaOH solution and then subject to measure its peak fluorescence intensity at 560 nm. The above procedure was repeated 10 times. In the above process, added an appropriate high concentration of HSPB-GQD to ensure that the HSPB-GQD concentration in the system is always 1.0 mg mL−1. The results were shown in Fig. 6. Fig. 6 shows that HSPB-GQD is still able to recover the fluorescence intensity at pH 4.5 and 10 after ten cycles, indicating a good reversibility. Therefore, HSPB-GQD can be used to detect pH value change reversibly with a high repeatability.

To test the practicability of HSPB-GQD for fluorescence detection of pH value, the fluorescence spectra of HSPB-GQD under Vis excitation of 480 nm were measured in a BR buffer with different pH values. As shown in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence intensity will decrease with the increase of pH value. This is because the increase of pH value caused a florescence quenching process of HSPB-GQD. The peak fluorescence intensity (F) at 560 nm vs. pH value has a good linear relationship at pH 4.5–10. The corresponding linear regression equation is: the F = -89696pH + 1000000, with the coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.995. This curve shows a very steep slope, verifying that the proposed method has a high sensitivity for sensing pH value. Storage stability of HSPB-GQD solution was tested by storing the sensor at 4 °C. Then, take it out every seven days and measure its response toward the BR buffer of pH 7 by using the same procedure. Three three-week storage period only produces 4% change in the peak fluorescence intensity, indicating good storage stability.

Biological and environmental water samples may contain some organic small molecules and inorganic anions and cations, which may interfere with the sensing pH value. To test the selectivity, the interference of the probe HSPB-GQD (0.2 mg mL−1) for pH detection was studied in the BR buffer of pH 7. The interfering species includes 1.0 mM of ascorbic acid (AA), urea, glucose (Glu), threonine (Thr), glycine (Gly) and arginine (Arg), and 0.5 mM of Na+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cl-, HCO3 – and HPO4 2-. Fig. 8 shows the sensing pH value by the probe HSPB-GQD is hardly affected by interference species. The result demonstrates that the proposed method has a good anti-interference ability for pH detection. It can be directly used for detection of pH value in water samples.

The proposed method has been applied to detection of pH value in three biological water samples and three environmental water samples. Three biological water samples including saliva, sweat and tears were collected from three volunteers in Jiangnan University. Three environmental water samples including lake water, rainwater and tap water were collected from Wuxi city. All samples were stored in refrigerator at 4 °C before used. The pH values of these samples were detected by the proposed method and pH meter, respectively. The results were shown in Table 1 . Table 1 indicates that the results obtained by proposed method are consistent with that obtained by pH meter, verifying that the proposed method has a high reliability for sensing pH value.

Based on unique luminescence behavior, the feasibility of HSPB-GQD can be used as a fluorescence probe for cell two-color imaging. Before the cell imaging application, the cytotoxicity of HSPB-GQD was investigated by MTT assay in Hela cells. A negatable toxicity was observed even when the concentration of HSPB-GQD is as high as 300 μg mL−1, indicating a good biocompatibility of HSPB-GQD. However, HSPB-GQD exhibits some toxic effect and a reduce in cell viability at 400 μg mL−1 exposure.

The application of HSPB-GQD in cellular imaging was examined by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) under visible light excitation of 480 nm (yellow CLSM). The Hela cells were incubated in the culture medium containing HSPB-GQD suspension and then were used for cell imaging. Fig. 9 shows the Yellow CLSM images of Hela cells incubated in HSPB-GQD for different times. The CLSM image appears strong yellow fluorescence when the incubation time is about 1 h that is significantly less than 6 h in the blue CLSM with common GQD (shown in Fig. s7 ). This is attributed to the strong yellow fluorescence emission of HSPB-GQD. In addition, the yellow fluorescence has a better penetration ability compared with blue fluorescence. This further helps to improve the yellow CLSM.

The study demonstrated the design and synthesis of histidine, serine and pentaethylenehexamine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot. Synergy of histidine, serine, pentaethylenehexamine-functionalized and boron atom creates a unique luminescence behavior. The 370 nm UV excitation produces one strongest blue fluorescence emission and 480 nm visible excitation produces one strongest yellow fluorescence emission. The two-color fluorescence emission can be sensitively switched by varying the excitation wavelength. The graphene quantum dot has been successfully applied sensing pH in biological and environmental water samples and cell imaging. The study also provides one way for design and synthesis of various functional graphene quantum dots in biosensing, fluorescence imaging, biomedicine, energy storage and conversion and catalysis.
Li Ruiyi: Investigation. Zhang Huahua: Investigation. Li Zaijun: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M702770) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121028.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Size Effect of graphene quantum dots on photoluminescence
